comments.body: is it possible that "path" is not the same as "PATH"? And adding to the path is not the same as setting/overwriting it (missing what already was there, like some Windows folder [e.g. C:\Windows\system32])

doskey is a Windows utility that has nothing to do with Eclispe, Java, or even programming. It's essentially legacy, as all terminals can do the same, far better. It's already in the PATH, so the only way to get that error is for something to try and call it with the wrong absolute path

There are a lot of issues in the code itself. You don't need to start a shell only to start another process, on Linux, MacOS or Windows. That means you don't need to call cmd.exe if you really want to run lasindex64.exe. Nothing in your code calls doskey though, so I suspect that lasindex64, whatever it is, does weird things, like using obsolete commands. And one of those weird things is trying to run doskey
( or lasindex64 is a batch script )
@user85421 "path" is what I found when I searched for how to add temporary variables and it is working on Win 10, just not Win 11.

@Neph but from Windows it is usually all UPPERCASE (and Java usually is case-sensitive, not sure what ProcessBuilder does regarding that - apparently it IS indeed (re-)setting PATH, but missing previous value [as given in posted code]) Why don't you put the command path in the command string, that is, use the full path for the command instead of changing the PATH variable

@Neph PATH isn't a temporary variable. In all OSs it plays the same role. And the error isn't about lasindex64 at all, it's about an obsolete Windows utility that's already in the PATH. You'll have to explain what is actually going on, as nothing here is about Java, Eclipse and something is breaking the PATH, either inside lasindex64 or when executing your code.

@PanagiotisKanavos Yes, lasindex64 (an .exe file that is part of LAStools) is (most likely) calling doskey - I don't know why it's doing that or where because I don't have access to the source files. LAStools only comes with console commands and I'm using the ProcessBuilder to call those, which also includes parameters like the paths/names of the files I want to process with the library (the commands can get long-ish, depending on what you want to do). All of this was working fine in Win 10 but it's not with Win 11, so something must have changed.

it is working on Win 10, just not Win 11. that means something on the machine is different. The PATH behavior hasn't changed since the 1980s. And this is probably the first time I've seen doskey used in almost 30 years - Windows don't use DOS

@user85421 Just tested it with "PATH" instead - same error. Before I add lasbinpath, PATH is null, afterwards it contains the added path to the library's "bin" folder, that's it, no "system32" or anything like that (which is in the actual environmen variable PATH).

an .exe file then the code is already bad. You don't need cmd to run another application or modify the path to call it. You can just pass the path to the executable, eg new ProcessBuilder("omepath\\LAStools\\bin\lasindex64.exe","-i",‌​"someotherpath\\myfi‌​le.laz");. If you have to specify a different working folder, use eg pb.directory(new File("somepath\\LAStools\\bin")); or pb.directory(new File("someotherpath"));.

@Neph if you keep trying random things you'll break something in the end. This isn't about Windows. PATH is null no way unless something's probably unusual with the way you start the application itself. As the ProcessBuilder docs say the created process gets an environment, which is a system-dependent mapping from variables to values. The initial value is a copy of the environment of the current process (see System.getenv()).. That includes the PATH. How are you running the applicatino?

@PanagiotisKanavos that means something on the machine is different - That exactly is the weird thing about this because everything should be set up the same way: Same Java, same Eclipse with the same settings, same library version, same code. The old PC had more PATH entries but nothing that would have any connection to this and nothing about doskey either. How are you running the applicatino? - I clean the project in Eclipse, then run the Main.java in Eclipse.
@PanagiotisKanavos I didn't miss your previous comment about calling the library in a different way, I'm going to test it in a bit.

null probably means use the default one ... you need System.getenv() to get the PATH... and, just checked, it also accepts lowercase path (as does ProcessBuilder) - it is converted to uppercase ← not the problem || setting path removes the previous value (just tested to be sure; not setting it at all → the actual process one is used [System.getenv("PATH")])

( I would start with trying String command = "somepath\\LAStools\\bin\\lasindex64 -i someotherpath\\myfile.laz"; ... actually I would also try WITHOUT cmd.exe if lasindex64 is not a batch file. that is, if it is lasindex64.exe )

I just checked the environment variables: There are no new "path" or "PATH" entries (so nothing was added). The actual spelling for PATH in the Windows settings is "Path" (capital "P", rest is lower case) and I can access its actual entries with env.get("Path") (system32, the lasbinpath that I added manually,...) - doesn't work with any other lower-/uppercase version. I removed env.put just in case and now it's working all of a sudden, no more error. Is it possible that "path" (env.put("path", libpfad_las)) masks the original "Path"? Why would this still run fine on Win 10 but not 11?

AGAIN 1) it is NOT the case as I wrote ("it also accepts lowercase path"); and 2) no environment set in ProcessBuilder (returning null) means to use the actual Java process one, the one returned by System.getenv (I tested that)

@user85421 Do I understand this correctly: env.get("path") doesn't use the default one ("Path") but creates a new one for that specific Java process (so a temporary one) that is of course empty, apart from "lasbinpath"? That's also what prints like System.out.println(env.get("path")); suggest (before: null, then the correct "lasbinpath").

Interesting, if I use System.getenv("path"), then the case sensitivity indeed doesn't matter, I get the same result with "path", "Path" and "PATH" (full list of entries). With env.get("Path") I have to spell it exactly that way, otherwise it returns null (for "path" and "PATH"). I never tested the first version on Win 10 but the second acted the same way there too.
env is a normal Map in Java, it sure is case-sensitive; getenv() is a method (native?) that (apparently) ignores the case

Thanks for the help with this to both of you, at least this is now working (haven't tested put with the real one yet). I'm going to test the other version of calling the process later, which still leaves one question: Why did it work in Win 10 but not in 11, even though they're both set up the same way?
( hint: often I write a test.cmd or similar that append attributes and/or variable(s) to a LOG file so I can see if it is being called and with which setting(s) - jshell is also very helpful )

@Panagiotis Kanavos — Sorry for off-topic... I periodically face the problem that I have trouble explaining to people why they don't need cmd and that is has nothing to do with Windows batch files. The inquirers on different platforms and even some of my colleagues cannot understand the trap, and I don't know how to convince them.
I just tested it: String path = env.get("Path"); env.put("Path", path+(path.endsWith(";")?"":";")+lasbinpath); (you have to append it, otherwise it overwrites the full entry) works on Win 11!

To all: The Microsoft documentation page Environment Variables describes the management of environment variables on Windows which are case-insensitive. It does not matter if path, Path or PATH is used to get the string value of the environment variable defined in local memory of a running process with PATH or path or Path. The Windows functions GetEnvironmentVariable and SetEnvironmentVariable are used by the Windows version of Java on using the Java functions to get or put an env variable.

env.put("path", lasbinpath); is a very bad idea because of that replaces the environment variable PATH passed from Windows shell to Eclipse and next to Java in local environment variables memory of java.exe by somepath\LAStools\bin. The PATH environment variable beginning with folder path C:\WINDOWS\system32 containing doskey.exe is not searched anymore by any executable started by java.exe after executing env.put("path", lasbinpath). There is now searched only somepath\LAStools\bin for libraries and executables which is not good.

There should be read further: What is the reason for "X is not recognized as an internal or external command, operable program or batch file"? This very long answer explains in full details how environment variables are managed on Windows on the most important environment variable PATH. There should also be read the Microsoft documentation of the Windows kernel library function CreateProcess.

The kernel function CreateProcess is called by ProcessBuilder of Java for Windows. CreateProcess is also called by cmd.exe to run an executable like lasindex64.exe. It is therefore completely useless to use ProcessBuilder to call CreateProcess to run cmd.exe with the option /c to run a command line and then close itself just for letting cmd.exe call CreateProcess to run lasindex64.exe with its arguments. ProcessBuilder can be used to call CreateProcess directly for the execution of lasindex64.exe.

Java Class ProcessBuilder offers public ProcessBuilder directory(File directory) to define the current working directory for the executable which ProcessBuilder passes to CreateProcess with the function parameter lpCurrentDirectory. It is therefore possible to make the directory of lasindex64.exe the current working directory for this executable if it is not good coded and expects its program directory as current directory to work at all.

cmd.exe calls CreateProcess with a null pointer for the function parameter lpEnvironment resulting in CreateProcess copies the current environment variables list of cmd.exe into the local memory of started lasindex64.exe. ProcessBuilder calls also CreateProcess with a null pointer for lpEnvironment, except public Map<String,String> environment() is used to get the current environment variables list, modify one or more environment variables in this list as done by the posted code for PATH environment variable and then start the process with that modified variables list.

The posted code replaces the PATH environment variable defined by the Windows shell (explorer.exe) passed to eclipse.exe passed further to java.exe by the single folder path somepath\LAStools\bin which is a bad idea as now the executable lasindex64.exe searches only in this directory for doskey.exe and not anymore in %SystemRoot%\System32. A solution would be env.put("path", lasbinpath + ";" + env.get("path")); or env.put("path", env.get("path") + ";" + lasbinpath); to prepend or append somepath\LAStools\bin to PATH.

Note: While environment variables are case-insensitive on Windows, the Java class Map interprets the key string case-sensitive as described by the Java documentation for the class ProcessBuilder for environment with the sentence: The returned map is typically case-sensitive on all platforms. So if it is really necessary to prepend or append lasbinpath to the environment variable PATH for lasindex64.exe, it is necessary to find out with which spelling PATH is in this map.

Last look on my comments below the question Qt's Qprocess to run CMD gives error of DOSKEY if the error message is output because of running cmd.exe instead of directly lasindex64.exe. There is a malicious AutoRun registry string value if that error message is output on running cmd.exe with environment variable PATH redefined before with just the folder path somepath\LAStools\bin. The malicious AutoRun registry value should be deleted as soon as possible as it affects lots of programs.

answers.body:
As Panagiotis has said, you don't need either cmd.exe or to mess around with PATH. Assuming you're returning textual data, you just need something like:

   public static List<String> extractLAS() throws IOException {
        List<String> command = List.of(
                "somepath\\LAStools\\bin\\lasindex64",
                "-i",
                "someotherpath\\myfile.laz");
        ProcessBuilder pb = new ProcessBuilder(command);
        try (Scanner s = new Scanner(pb.start().getInputStream()).useDelimiter("\\R")) {
            return s.tokens().toList();
        }
    }

title: "command is either misspelled or could not be found" with Win 11 but not Win 10
body:
A year ago I created an Eclipse project (Eclipse 2020-06, Java 8, Win 10) that uses the GIS LAStools libary to e.g. extract data from a couple of files.

I'm now using Win 11 on a different PC and want to work on the project again, so I copied the old project, installed the same Java version, the same Eclipse version (with the same settings, as far as I've seen) and the same library version.

My code:

Process p = null;
ArrayList<String> lines = new ArrayList<String>();
String lasbinpath = "somepath\\LAStools\\bin";
String command = "lasindex64 -i someotherpath\\myfile.laz";

try {
    ProcessBuilder builder = new ProcessBuilder("cmd.exe", "/c", command);
    Map<String, String> env = builder.environment();
    env.put("path", lasbinpath);
    builder.redirectErrorStream(true);
    p = builder.start();
    BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(p.getInputStream()));
    String line;

    while(true) {
        line = br.readLine();
        if (line==null) { break; }
        lines.add(line);
    }
    br.close();
} catch(IOException e) {
    e.printStackTrace();
} finally {
    if(p!=null) { p.destroy(); }
}

When I try to run it, there's always the same error:

The "DOSKEY" command is either misspelled or could not be found.

At first I thought that it might be the library's fault and started a question in the GIS stackexchange.

Yesterday I started up the old Win 10 PC again and the same code is still working fine there, even with the latest version of the library.

On Win 11 the same error appears when I run the code in Eclipse 2025-09 with OpenJDK 24 (even when run as admin) but there's no error when I run the library's command directly in command prompt. The "doskey.exe" file does exist in the "System32" folder and while the "Windows" and "System32" folders are protected, of course, I can manually access them without any problems. Other (native) commands like java -version and dir work fine with my code.

If I remove env.put("path", lasbinpath) and manually add the path to PATH instead, there's no more error and the code is working as expected.

I know that Win 11 can be weird about permissions and I've had problems with those before (app that was working fine on Win 10 can't access files in Win 11, unless run as admin), so I think that this is the case here too.

There are a couple of google search results about things not working as expected on Win 11, compared to Win 10 (not just with Java) but nothing about the ProcessBuilder. Is there a specific way I have to set up Eclipse and/or ProcessBuilder to give them access and work with a temporary PATH?
